title: Auditory Fine Arts
intro: Auditory Fine Arts questions in quizbowl draw overwhelmingly on the Western classical tradition, spanning roughly nine centuries from Hildegard von Bingen and Gregorian chant to living composers such as John Adams and Kaija Saariaho. The heaviest concentration of tossups falls on the Classical-into-Romantic core: Mozart, Beethoven, Brahms, Schubert, and Schumann anchor the German-Austrian symphonic and Lied traditions, while Bach, Handel, and Vivaldi represent the Baroque, and Haydn bridges the two eras. Opera forms a second major axis that cuts across every period, from Monteverdi's L'Orfeo and Handel's oratorios through Mozart's comic operas, Verdi and Puccini's Italian repertoire, Wagner's music dramas, and twentieth-century works by Britten and Glass.

A large share of the canon reflects composers who wrote in an explicitly national or folk idiom: the Russian Mighty Five (Mussorgsky, Rimsky-Korsakov, Borodin), Czech nationalists Dvorak and Smetana, Scandinavian figures Grieg and Sibelius, Hungarian pioneers Bartok and Kodaly, and English pastoral composers Vaughan Williams, Holst, and Elgar. Early twentieth-century French Impressionism (Debussy, Ravel) and the Ballets Russes collaborations of Stravinsky feed into a broader modernist cluster built around the Second Viennese School (Schoenberg, Berg, Webern) and later minimalists (Reich, Glass, Adams), who remain frequent answers in questions on contemporary concert music.

Beyond named composers and works, a substantial fraction of answerlines are generic: individual instruments (violin, clarinet, piano, harpsichord), musical forms and theory terms (sonata form, fugue, rondo, sonata, symphony, string quartet), and the orchestras, halls, and cities most associated with performance history (Vienna, Leipzig, Boston, Carnegie Hall). A distinct but smaller cluster covers American vernacular music, principally jazz, running from Armstrong and Ellington through Parker, Davis, and Coltrane, along with ragtime, ragtime-adjacent popular song, ballet, and Broadway musical theater, reflecting the unit's secondary emphasis on American concert and popular idioms alongside its European classical core.

sections.name: Medieval and Renaissance
sections.blurb: Chant, polyphony, and early vocal music from Hildegard through Palestrina, Byrd, and the Renaissance madrigalists and motet composers.

entries.note: English Renaissance composer of Tudor sacred music, including the 40-voice motet Spem in Alium.
Secular vocal part-songs of the Renaissance, especially Italian and English; Monteverdi's books span the Renaissance-Baroque transition.
Italian Renaissance composer of the Roman School, master of clear vocal polyphony; wrote the Pope Marcellus Mass.
Monophonic, unaccompanied liturgical chant of the medieval Catholic Church, the foundation of Western notated music.
Franco-Flemish Renaissance composer renowned for masses and motets admired across Europe.
English Renaissance composer and organist, a Catholic under Protestant rule known for masses and keyboard music.
French medieval poet-composer of the Ars Nova, known for the Messe de Nostre Dame.
Latin sacred text on Mary's sorrow at the crucifixion, set by composers from Palestrina to Pergolesi.
Medieval German abbess and composer of monophonic sacred chant, among the earliest named composers.
English Renaissance lutenist-composer known for melancholic songs like "Flow, My Tears."
Medieval music theorist who developed staff notation and the solfege syllables (ut-re-mi).
Protestant reformer who wrote German-language hymns, including "A Mighty Fortress is Our God."
Hymn written by Martin Luther, based on Psalm 46 and central to Lutheran chorale tradition.
Secular vocal part-song of the Renaissance, especially in Italy and England, often setting pastoral or love poetry.
Earliest known form of Western polyphony, adding a second voice to plainchant in medieval practice.
Popular secular tune used as a cantus firmus in dozens of Renaissance mass settings.
14th-century French musical movement associated with Machaut, introducing rhythmic innovations in notation.
Italian Renaissance nobleman-composer known for extremely chromatic madrigals and for murdering his wife.
Early Renaissance Burgundian composer known for masses and motets, among the era's most influential figures.
Franco-Flemish Renaissance composer celebrated for intricate polyphonic masses and canonic technique.
Variant rendering of Josquin des Prez, the Franco-Flemish Renaissance master of masses and motets.
Variant spelling of Dufay, the early Renaissance Burgundian master of masses and motets.
Variant name for the medieval German abbess-composer of monophonic sacred chant.
Extremely complex, rhythmically intricate late-medieval notational style following the Ars Nova.
Venetian Renaissance composer known for polychoral works exploiting St. Mark's Basilica's architecture.
Shortened name for the Italian Renaissance master of clear vocal polyphony.
Misspelled variant of Palestrina, the Italian Renaissance master of clear vocal polyphony.
Italian Renaissance-Baroque composer of the famously guarded choral work Miserere mei, Deus.
Paris cathedral that gave its name to the medieval Notre Dame school of early polyphony.
Full name of the Paris cathedral tied to the medieval Notre Dame school of organum composers Leonin and Perotin.
Thomas Tallis's monumental 40-voice motet, a showpiece of Tudor sacred polyphony.
Full opening words of the Latin sacred text on Mary's sorrow, set by Palestrina, Pergolesi, and others.
Monophonic, unaccompanied liturgical singing, especially the Gregorian chant of the medieval Catholic Church.
Early Renaissance Burgundian composer and contemporary of Dufay, known for chansons.
Plural of the monophonic, unaccompanied liturgical chant of the medieval Catholic Church.
Medieval technique alternating rapid notes and rests between voices to create a hiccuping effect.
Medieval composer of the Notre Dame school, a pioneer of measured polyphonic organum.
Gregorio Allegri's famously guarded choral setting of Psalm 51, long restricted to Sistine Chapel performance.
English Renaissance composer known for keyboard, consort, and Anglican church music.
Medieval French theorist and composer associated with the rhythmic innovations of the Ars Nova.
Palestrina's celebrated mass, long credited with saving polyphony from Counter-Reformation reformers.
Spanish Renaissance composer of intensely expressive sacred polyphony.
Medieval poet-musicians of courtly love active in southern France.

sections.name: Baroque
sections.blurb: Bach, Handel, Vivaldi, and the rise of tonal counterpoint, concerto grosso, fugue, and other instrumental forms of the 17th and early 18th centuries.

entries.note: German Baroque master of counterpoint; wrote the Brandenburg Concertos, Well-Tempered Clavier, and Mass in B minor.
German-born English Baroque composer of Messiah, Water Music, and numerous opera serias and oratorios.
Italian Baroque violinist-composer famed for The Four Seasons and hundreds of concertos; taught at the Ospedale della Pieta.
Italian Baroque violinist-composer who standardized the concerto grosso and trio sonata forms.
Prolific German Baroque composer, a contemporary and friend of Bach known for his enormous instrumental output.
Italian Baroque composer at the Spanish court, known for hundreds of one-movement harpsichord sonatas.
Bach's set of keyboard variations on an aria, reputedly written to soothe an insomniac count.
Handel's suite of orchestral movements composed for a royal barge procession on the Thames.
French Baroque harpsichordist-composer known as "le Grand," author of a treatise on harpsichord playing.
Vivaldi's set of four violin concertos, each depicting a season with programmatic imagery in accompanying sonnets.
Handel's English-language oratorio on the life of Christ, famous for its "Hallelujah" chorus.
Italian Baroque surname shared by Domenico, composer of harpsichord sonatas, and his father Alessandro.
Bach's set of six concertos for varied instrumental combinations, dedicated to the Margrave of Brandenburg.
Bach's set of contrapuntal pieces built on a theme given to him by Frederick the Great.
Bach's two books of preludes and fugues in all 24 major and minor keys.
German Baroque organist whose work at Lubeck influenced the young J.S. Bach.
Abbreviated name of the German Baroque master of counterpoint and composer of the Brandenburg Concertos.
Prussian king and amateur flutist who gave Bach the theme used in The Musical Offering.
German Baroque composer best known for the widely arranged Canon in D.
Bach's monumental setting of the Latin mass, compiled near the end of his life.
Handel's suite composed for a royal fireworks display celebrating a peace treaty.
Bach's large-scale setting of Christ's passion narrative from the Gospel of Matthew.
Italian Baroque singer-composer known for a large output of secular cantatas and arias.
French Baroque surname most associated with harpsichordist-composer Francois Couperin "le Grand."
Italian Baroque composer who died young, remembered for the Stabat Mater and comic intermezzo La serva padrona.
English Baroque composer and organist, teacher of Henry Purcell at Westminster Abbey.
French Baroque composer whose Te Deum prelude became famous as the Eurovision broadcast fanfare.
Bach's concerto featuring a famously high trumpet part alongside recorder, oboe, and violin.
Full title of the French Baroque harpsichordist-composer known as "le Grand."
Spelling variant of the German-born English Baroque composer of Messiah.
Spelling variant of the German-born English Baroque composer of Messiah.
Spelling variant of the German-born English Baroque composer of Messiah.
Italian Baroque violinist-composer known for the "Devil's Trill" Sonata.
Dutch composer-organist bridging Renaissance and Baroque styles, influential on North German organ music.
Austrian Baroque composer and theorist whose treatise Gradus ad Parnassum codified counterpoint pedagogy.
Heinrich Biber's violin sonatas using scordatura tuning to depict mysteries of the rosary.

entries.answerline: Toccata and Fugue in D minor
entries.note: Bach's dramatic organ work, among the most famous pieces in the organ repertoire.
Italian Baroque composer of operas and cantatas, father of harpsichordist Domenico Scarlatti.
Second wife of J.S. Bach, namesake of a notebook of keyboard pieces compiled for her.
Full name of the Italian Baroque violinist-composer famed for The Four Seasons.
Surname of the German Baroque family dominated by Johann Sebastian Bach, master of counterpoint.

Musical period spanning roughly 1600-1750, defined by counterpoint, ornamentation, and basso continuo.

Bach's comic secular cantata satirizing a daughter's coffee addiction and her father's disapproval.
Spelling variant of the German-born English Baroque composer of Messiah.

Austrian Baroque violinist-composer known for the scordatura-based Rosary Sonatas.
Handel's oratorio celebrating a Jewish military hero's triumph, including "See, the Conquering Hero Comes."
Vivaldi's influential collection of twelve concertos that shaped the genre across Europe.

Italian Baroque violinist-composer known for extremely virtuosic caprices.
"Church sonata" genre of slow-fast movement alternation, contrasted with the dance-based sonata da camera.
Bach's dramatic setting of Christ's passion narrative from the Gospel of John.
Bach's late, unfinished cycle exhaustively exploring contrapuntal possibilities of a single theme.
Nickname for a movement from one of Handel's harpsichord suites.
Italian Baroque composer whose name is attached to a famous "Adagio," largely a 20th-century reconstruction.

sections.name: Classical Era
sections.blurb: Haydn, Mozart, and early Beethoven establish the symphony, string quartet, and sonata form in Vienna.

entries.note: Austrian child prodigy and leading Classical composer of Don Giovanni, The Magic Flute, and 41 symphonies.
German composer bridging Classical and Romantic eras; nine symphonies, the opera Fidelio, and 32 piano sonatas.
Austrian "father of the symphony and string quartet"; longtime Esterhazy court composer, wrote the oratorio The Creation.
Austrian Classical composer, mentor to Mozart and teacher of Beethoven; prolific symphonist and string quartet pioneer.
Italian Classical-era cellist-composer active in Spain, best known for a famous Minuet from a string quintet.
Austrian pianist-composer, a pupil of Mozart, bridging Classical style toward early Romanticism.
Italian-English Classical pianist-composer known for pedagogical sonatinas and piano method books.
Austrian pianist-pedagogue who studied with Beethoven and taught Liszt, remembered for his piano etudes.
Beethoven's Symphony No. 3, originally dedicated to Napoleon before he crowned himself emperor.

entries.topic: piano sonatas by Ludwig van Beethoven
entries.answerline: piano sonatas by Ludwig van Beethoven
entries.note: Beethoven's cycle of 32 piano sonatas, spanning his entire career from Classical style to late innovation.
Haydn's oratorio depicting the biblical creation story, inspired by his encounter with Handel's oratorios in London.
Italian composer at the Viennese court, a contemporary and rumored rival of Mozart, later Beethoven's teacher.

entries.topic: piano sonatas by Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart
entries.answerline: piano sonatas by Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart
entries.note: Mozart's cycle of 18 piano sonatas, including the famous C major "Sonata facile."
Son of J.S. Bach whose expressive "empfindsamer Stil" keyboard works influenced Haydn and early Classical style.
Nickname for Beethoven's Piano Sonata No. 14, its famous opening movement a hushed, triplet-based reverie.

entries.topic: piano concertos by Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart
entries.answerline: piano concertos by Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart
entries.note: Mozart's cycle of 27 piano concertos, central to the genre's Classical-era development.
Nickname for Haydn's Symphony No. 45, whose musicians leave the stage one by one at its close.
Nickname for Haydn's Symphony No. 94, featuring a sudden loud chord in its otherwise quiet slow movement.
Nickname for Mozart's final Symphony No. 41, celebrated for its contrapuntal finale.
Composer-violinist and pedagogue, father and first teacher of Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart.
Haydn's final set of twelve symphonies, written for triumphant concert visits to London.
Beethoven's cycle of nine symphonies, expanding the form's scale and emotional range across his career.
Haydn's oratorio depicting rural life through the four seasons, a companion to his earlier Creation.
Austrian Classical composer and violinist, a contemporary of Haydn and Mozart.
Beethoven's famous short piano bagatelle, dedicated to an unidentified "Elise."
Founder of the Mannheim school and orchestra, a key precursor to the Classical symphonic style.
Nickname for Haydn's Symphony No. 100, featuring percussion suggesting Turkish military bands.
Nickname for Beethoven's Symphony No. 6, evoking scenes of countryside and a passing thunderstorm.

entries.topic: string quartets by Franz Joseph Haydn
entries.answerline: string quartets by Franz Joseph Haydn
entries.note: Haydn's pioneering body of string quartets that established the genre's four-movement form.

entries.topic: string quartets by Ludwig van Beethoven

One of Mozart's final symphonies, notable for its urgent minor-key opening theme.
Beethoven's final, "Choral" symphony, setting Schiller's "Ode to Joy" for vocal soloists and chorus.
Choral finale theme of Beethoven's Symphony No. 9, setting Schiller's poem on universal brotherhood.

Abbreviated name of J.S. Bach's son, whose expressive keyboard style influenced early Classical composers.

Beethoven's massive, dissonant fugal movement, originally the finale of a late string quartet.
Beethoven's demanding Violin Sonata No. 9, dedicated to violinist Rodolphe Kreutzer.
Austrian Classical composer, younger brother of Joseph Haydn, active in Salzburg sacred music.
Set of six symphonies Haydn composed on commission for Parisian concert audiences.
Nickname for Beethoven's Piano Sonata No. 8, notable for its dramatic minor-key intensity.

entries.topic: piano concertos by Ludwig van Beethoven
entries.answerline: piano concertos by Ludwig van Beethoven
entries.note: Beethoven's five piano concertos, including the popular "Emperor" Concerto.
Full name of the Italian Classical-era cellist-composer known for a famous Minuet.
Mozart's output of 41 numbered symphonies, culminating in the "Jupiter."
Beethoven's "Pastoral" Symphony, evoking scenes of countryside and a passing thunderstorm.
Beethoven's monumental set of piano variations on a simple waltz theme by publisher Anton Diabelli.
Mozart's cycle of 27 piano concertos, central to the genre's Classical-era development.

sections.name: Romanticism
sections.blurb: Nineteenth-century German and Austrian symphonists, virtuoso pianists, and song composers who expanded harmony, orchestral scale, and emotional expression.

entries.note: German Romantic composer of four symphonies, the German Requiem, and dense chamber and piano works; seen as Beethoven's heir.
German Romantic composer of piano cycles like Carnaval and Kinderszenen and song cycles; married pianist Clara Schumann.
Hungarian virtuoso pianist-composer of the Hungarian Rhapsodies and symphonic poems; pioneered thematic transformation.
Austrian composer of over 600 lieder and the "Unfinished" and "Great" symphonies; died young at 31.
Austrian late-Romantic composer of nine numbered symphonies and song cycles like Das Lied von der Erde; also a leading conductor.
Polish-French Romantic composer almost exclusively for solo piano; wrote nocturnes, mazurkas, polonaises, and etudes.
French Romantic composer of the programmatic Symphonie Fantastique, known for innovative orchestration and the idee fixe device.
Late-Romantic Russian composer-pianist known for lush piano concertos and the Rhapsody on a Theme of Paganini.
French Romantic composer of Carnival of the Animals, the Organ Symphony, and the opera Samson and Delilah.
German early-Romantic composer of the Italian and Scottish symphonies, Elijah, and the Midsummer Night's Dream incidental music.
Italian virtuoso violinist-composer famed for dazzling technique in his 24 Caprices; inspired Liszt's pianistic virtuosity.
German late-Romantic composer of the tone poems Also sprach Zarathustra and Don Juan and the operas Salome and Der Rosenkavalier.
Austrian late-Romantic symphonist known for massive, devout symphonies influenced by Wagner's harmony.
Early German Romantic composer whose opera Der Freischutz helped launch German Romantic opera.
Italian composer of the orchestral tone poems Pines of Rome and Fountains of Rome, known for lush, colorful orchestration.
Belgian-French Romantic organist-composer known for the cyclic Symphony in D minor and a Violin Sonata.
Full name of the Austrian composer of over 600 lieder and the "Unfinished" Symphony.
French Romantic composer known for a serene Requiem and refined art songs and chamber music.
German Romantic composer best known for his Violin Concerto No. 1 and the cello-and-orchestra piece Kol Nidrei.
Robert Schumann's piano cycle of character pieces representing masked figures and fellow musicians.
German Romantic pianist-composer and Robert Schumann's wife, a leading concert virtuoso of her era.
American Romantic virtuoso pianist-composer who blended Creole and Caribbean idioms into concert works.
Saint-Saens's suite of humorous musical portraits of animals, including "The Swan" for cello.
Full name of the German early-Romantic composer of the Italian and Scottish symphonies.
Richard Strauss's tone poem after Nietzsche, its opening famous from 2001: A Space Odyssey.
Full name of the late-Romantic Russian composer-pianist known for lush piano concertos.
Mendelssohn's collection of lyrical, wordless piano character pieces published across several volumes.
Schubert's song cycle following a rejected lover's bleak winter journey, set to poems by Wilhelm Muller.

Nickname for Mahler's Symphony No. 2, ending with a massive choral setting on death and rebirth.
Mendelssohn's overture and incidental music for Shakespeare's comedy, including the famous "Wedding March."
Richard Strauss's tone poem depicting a day's mountain ascent and descent in vivid orchestral detail.
Schumann's song cycle setting Heine's poems on the pain and joy of love.
Schubert's song cycle following a miller's apprentice through unrequited love and eventual despair.

Grieg's suite for string orchestra written in homage to the Danish-Norwegian writer Ludvig Holberg.
Austrian composer renowned for intensely expressive art songs, especially settings of Morike and Goethe.
German-Hungarian virtuoso violinist and close friend of Brahms, who dedicated his Violin Concerto to him.
Berlioz's programmatic symphony depicting an artist's opium-fueled obsession, unified by a recurring idee fixe.

Nickname for Schubert's String Quartet No. 14, based on his earlier song of the same name.
French Romantic composer best known for the Spanish-flavored violin showpiece Symphonie espagnole.
Richard Strauss's autobiographical tone poem depicting a hero's life and battles with his critics.
French Romantic composer known for the vibrant orchestral rhapsody España.
Chopin's four narrative-style piano works, blending virtuosity with dramatic, story-like structure.
Full name of the Polish-French Romantic composer for solo piano.
Austrian virtuoso violinist-composer known for short salon pieces, some published as pastiches of older masters.

Nickname for Saint-Saens's Symphony No. 3, featuring a prominent pipe organ part.
Respighi's orchestral tone poem depicting Roman pine trees in four different settings, including a recorded nightingale.

sections.name: Opera
sections.blurb: Opera composers, works, singers, and terminology spanning Monteverdi's earliest experiments through twentieth-century stage works, across every national tradition.

entries.note: Early Baroque composer of L'Orfeo (1607), among the first operas still regularly performed; bridged Renaissance madrigal and Baroque monody.
English Baroque composer of the opera Dido and Aeneas, noted for its lament "When I am laid in earth."
German composer of music dramas including the Ring cycle, Tristan und Isolde, and Lohengrin; pursued the Gesamtkunstwerk ideal.
French Baroque composer and theorist who wrote tragedies en musique and a landmark treatise on harmony.
Italian-born French Baroque composer who created French tragedie en musique under Louis XIV's patronage.

Richard Strauss's intense one-act opera on Greek myth's vengeful daughter of Agamemnon.
Wagner's opera of a mysterious swan knight, source of the wedding processional "Here Comes the Bride."
Mozart's Singspiel blending spoken dialogue and song, rich with Masonic symbolism.
Wagner's music drama of doomed love, famous for the harmonically ambiguous "Tristan chord."
Seductive duet between Don Giovanni and Zerlina from Mozart's Don Giovanni.
Dido's grief-stricken lament closing Purcell's opera Dido and Aeneas.
Verdi's grand opera set in ancient Egypt, commissioned for the opening of the Cairo Opera House.
Full name of the French Romantic opera composer best known for Faust.
Opening opera of Wagner's Ring cycle, introducing the cursed gold and the gods of Valhalla.
Weber's opera featuring a demonic bullet-casting scene, a landmark of German Romantic opera.
Bartok's only opera, a symbolic psychological drama of Bluebeard's new wife opening seven forbidden doors.
Gounod's opera adaptation of Goethe's drama, centered on a scholar's pact with the devil Mephistopheles.
Alban Berg's unfinished twelve-tone opera following a femme fatale's rise and destruction.
Common-link answerline spanning Monteverdi's L'Orfeo, Gluck's Orfeo ed Euridice, and Offenbach's Orpheus in the Underworld.
Verdi's late opera based on Shakespeare's Othello, marking a mature synthesis of drama and music.
Wagner's final music drama, a sacred stage-consecration festival play centered on the Holy Grail.
Debussy's only completed opera, a hushed, symbolist setting of Maeterlinck's play.
Tchaikovsky's opera based on Pushkin's tale of an obsessive gambler seeking a card-game secret.
Rossini's final opera, its overture a concert-hall favorite depicting a storm and cavalry charge.
Menotti's Christmas opera about a disabled boy visited by the Three Kings, written for television.
Richard Strauss's comic opera of an aging noblewoman relinquishing her young lover.
Wagner's comic opera of a song contest among Nuremberg guild singers.
Austrian operetta composer known for the lively overture to Light Cavalry.
Handel's opera seria depicting Julius Caesar's Egyptian campaign and romance with Cleopatra.
Lavish 19th-century French opera genre featuring spectacle and ballet, epitomized by Meyerbeer.
Gluck's reform opera simplifying opera seria conventions in a story of Greek myth and sacrifice.
Contemporary American opera composer known for Dead Man Walking, based on the memoir and film.
Gluck's reform opera simplifying earlier opera seria conventions in retelling the Orpheus myth.
Offenbach's satirical operetta retelling the Orpheus myth as comic spectacle, featuring the famous can-can.
Britten's opera of an outcast fisherman suspected in his apprentices' deaths, set in an English coastal town.
German-language genre alternating spoken dialogue with song, as in Mozart's Abduction from the Seraglio and The Magic Flute.
Massenet's opera about a courtesan's religious conversion, famous for its orchestral "Meditation."
Smetana's comic opera of a village girl's arranged marriage plot, a cornerstone of Czech national opera.
Mozart's comic opera continuing Beaumarchais's Figaro story, satirizing aristocratic privilege.
Offenbach's opera dramatizing the fantastical loves of writer E.T.A. Hoffmann.
Harmonically ambiguous chord opening Wagner's Tristan und Isolde, a landmark in the dissolution of tonality.
Puccini's final, unfinished opera set in legendary China, home to the aria "Nessun dorma."
Italian bel canto opera composer known for long, flowing melodic lines in works like Norma.
Full name of the German composer of music dramas including the Ring cycle and Tristan und Isolde.

sections.name: National Schools and Folk Traditions
sections.blurb: Composers who drew on folk material and national identity, from the Russian Mighty Five to Czech, Scandinavian, Iberian, English, and Latin American nationalists.

entries.note: Hungarian composer and ethnomusicologist who folded collected folk melodies into modernist works like the Concerto for Orchestra.
20th-century English composer of Peter Grimes, the War Requiem, and The Young Person's Guide to the Orchestra; founded the Aldeburgh Festival.
English composer who collected folk songs; wrote pastoral symphonies and Fantasia on a Theme by Thomas Tallis.
Finnish nationalist composer of seven symphonies and the tone poem Finlandia; became a symbol of Finnish independence.
Russian Romantic composer of the ballets Swan Lake and The Nutcracker, the 1812 Overture, and six symphonies.
Czech nationalist composer of the New World Symphony and Slavonic Dances, drawing on Czech and American folk idioms.
English composer best known for the orchestral suite The Planets; friend and contemporary of Vaughan Williams.
Norwegian nationalist composer of the Piano Concerto in A minor and the Peer Gynt suites.
Czech nationalist composer known for speech-melody-influenced operas like Jenufa and the orchestral Sinfonietta.
English composer of the Enigma Variations, the Cello Concerto in E minor, and Pomp and Circumstance marches.
Russian nationalist of the Mighty Five, composer of Scheherazade and the treatise on orchestration.
Czech nationalist composer of the symphonic cycle Ma vlast and the opera The Bartered Bride.
Brazilian nationalist composer who blended Bach-like counterpoint with native idioms in the Bachianas Brasileiras.
Mussorgsky's piano suite depicting an art exhibit, most famous today in Ravel's orchestration.
Danish nationalist symphonist known for six symphonies including the "Inextinguishable."
Hungarian composer and educator who collected folk songs alongside Bartok and devised a music-teaching method.
Armenian Soviet composer known for the ballet Gayane, including its popular "Sabre Dance."
Rimsky-Korsakov's orchestral suite depicting tales from the Arabian Nights, part of Russian musical Orientalism.
Russian nationalist of the Mighty Five, also a chemist, known for the opera Prince Igor and its Polovtsian Dances.
Smetana's cycle of six symphonic poems celebrating Czech landscape and legend, including "The Moldau."
Spanish nationalist composer known for Nights in the Gardens of Spain and the ballet El amor brujo.
Russian nationalist of the Mighty Five, composer of Pictures at an Exhibition and the opera Boris Godunov.
Full name of the Czech nationalist composer of the New World Symphony and Slavonic Dances.
English 20th-century composer known for the oratorio Belshazzar's Feast and a Viola Concerto.
Bela Bartok's late orchestral showpiece written in the United States, treating each section virtuosically.
Full name of the Russian nationalist of the Mighty Five, composer of Scheherazade.
Gustav Holst's orchestral suite depicting the astrological character of each planet, including "Mars, the Bringer of War."
Joaquin Rodrigo's guitar concerto, its slow movement among the most famous melodies in Spanish music.
Full name of the Norwegian nationalist composer of the Piano Concerto in A minor.
Elgar's orchestral variations portraying friends through musical "enigmas," including the famous "Nimrod."
Czech composer and violinist, son-in-law of Dvorak, who continued the Czech Romantic-nationalist tradition.
Russian composer often called the father of Russian classical music, wrote the opera A Life for the Tsar.
Spanish virtuoso violinist-composer known for Gypsy-flavored showpieces like Zigeunerweisen.
Australian-American composer who collected and arranged English folk songs like "Country Gardens."
Finnish composer following Sibelius, known for mystical works like Cantus Arcticus incorporating birdsong.
Full name of the Russian nationalist of the Mighty Five, composer of Pictures at an Exhibition.
English composer of African descent known for Hiawatha's Wedding Feast, blending Romantic style with African American idioms.
Dvorak's orchestral dance suites inspired by Brahms's Hungarian Dances, drawing on Czech and Slavic folk idioms.
Tchaikovsky's festival overture commemorating Russia's defense against Napoleon, ending with cannon fire and bells.
Villa-Lobos's suites blending Bach-like counterpoint with Brazilian folk melody and rhythm.
Full name of the Hungarian composer-ethnomusicologist who collected folk melodies into modernist works.
Sibelius's patriotic tone poem that became an unofficial anthem of Finnish independence.

entries.topic: In the Steppes of Central Asia
entries.answerline: In the Steppes of Central Asia
entries.note: Borodin's orchestral tone poem evoking a caravan crossing the Asian steppe under Russian escort.
Bartok's six-volume progressive piano collection infusing pedagogical pieces with Hungarian folk idiom.
Nickname for Tchaikovsky's Symphony No. 6, his final work, premiered days before his death.
Grieg's incidental music and suites for Ibsen's play, including "Morning Mood" and "In the Hall of the Mountain King."
Tchaikovsky's grand fairy-tale ballet, one of his three collaborations with choreographer Marius Petipa.
Britten's large-scale requiem interleaving the Latin mass with Wilfred Owen's World War I poetry.
English folk tune arranged by Vaughan Williams as the Fantasia on Greensleeves.
Argentine nationalist composer known for ballets and works drawing on gaucho and folk idioms.
Russian late-Romantic composer bridging the Mighty Five's nationalism and the Rimsky-Korsakov teaching lineage.
Full name of the Russian nationalist of the Mighty Five, composer of Prince Igor.
Argentine composer who created "nuevo tango," fusing tango with jazz and classical elements.
Rimsky-Korsakov's colorful orchestral showpiece evoking Spanish melodies and rhythms.
African American composer who wove spirituals and folk idiom into symphonic works, a first for a Black woman with a major orchestra.
Spanish nationalist composer known for the piano suite Iberia, evoking Spanish regional color.
Balakirev's virtuosic "Oriental fantasy" for piano, inspired by Caucasian folk melodies.
Percy Grainger's wind band suite based on English folk songs collected in Lincolnshire.
Tchaikovsky's programmatic symphony inspired by Byron's poem of a guilt-ridden nobleman.
Full name of the composer often called the father of Russian classical music.
Dvorak's Symphony No. 9, written in America and incorporating spiritual- and folk-inspired melodies.
Leos Janacek's brass-heavy orchestral work, originally composed for a gymnastics festival.
Full title of the English composer of the Enigma Variations and Pomp and Circumstance marches.
Elgar's oratorio setting Cardinal Newman's poem on a soul's journey after death.
Tchaikovsky's Christmas ballet featuring the Sugar Plum Fairy's celesta-accompanied dance.
Ominous, driving opening movement of Holst's orchestral suite The Planets.
Vaughan Williams's Symphony No. 2, evoking the sounds and atmosphere of London.
Villa-Lobos's series of works blending Brazilian popular and folk idiom with orchestral or chamber forces.

entries.topic: Edward Elgar's Cello Concerto in E Minor
entries.answerline: Edward Elgar's Cello Concerto in E Minor
entries.note: Elgar's elegiac late concerto, a cornerstone of the cello repertoire popularized by Jacqueline du Pre.
Grieg's ten books of short, evocative piano character pieces.
Sibelius's tone poem from the Lemminkainen Suite, depicting a swan on the river to the Finnish underworld.

entries.topic: The Young Person's Guide to the Orchestra
entries.answerline: The Young Person's Guide to the Orchestra
entries.note: Britten's educational orchestral work introducing instrument families through a Purcell theme.

entries.topic: "In the Hall of the Mountain King"
entries.answerline: "In the Hall of the Mountain King"
entries.note: Grieg's suspenseful, accelerating movement from his Peer Gynt incidental music.
Ethereal final movement of Holst's The Planets, featuring an offstage wordless women's choir.
William Walton's dramatic oratorio depicting the biblical king's downfall at a lavish banquet.
Czech 20th-century composer blending neoclassical style with Czech folk-inflected melody.
Elgar's elegiac late concerto, a cornerstone of the cello repertoire popularized by Jacqueline du Pre.

entries.topic: Fantasia on a Theme by Thomas Tallis
entries.answerline: Fantasia on a Theme by Thomas Tallis
entries.note: Vaughan Williams's string work built on a 16th-century psalm tune by Thomas Tallis.
Spanish guitarist-composer sometimes called the "father of classical guitar" repertoire.
Khachaturian's ballet, home to the propulsive "Sabre Dance."
English pastoral composer known for gentle, lyrical settings of English poetry.
Full name of the English composer best known for the orchestral suite The Planets.
African American composer-arranger of spirituals whose singing introduced Dvorak to that folk repertoire.
Isaac Albeniz's piano suite evoking the regional color of Spain.

entries.topic: Jean Sibelius's Violin Concerto in D minor
entries.answerline: Jean Sibelius's Violin Concerto in D minor
entries.note: Sibelius's only concerto, a technically demanding staple of the violin repertoire.
Full name of the Russian nationalist who led the Mighty Five.
Mussorgsky's vivid orchestral tone poem depicting a witches' sabbath.
Spelling variant of the Russian Romantic composer of Swan Lake and The Nutcracker.
Full name of the English 20th-century composer known for the oratorio Belshazzar's Feast.
Holst's suite for string orchestra written for the students of St. Paul's Girls' School.
Tchaikovsky's ballet of a princess transformed into a swan by a sorcerer's curse.
Sibelius's cycle of seven symphonies, moving from Romantic lyricism to compressed, distilled late style.
Holst's orchestral suite depicting the astrological character of each planet.

sections.name: Impressionism and the Ballets Russes
sections.blurb: French tone-color innovators and the Russian-French ballet collaborations that reshaped early twentieth-century orchestral color and rhythm.

entries.note: French composer of Bolero and Daphnis et Chloe, a master orchestrator often paired with Debussy in musical Impressionism.
French Impressionist composer of Prelude to the Afternoon of a Faun and La Mer; pioneered whole-tone harmony and tone color.
Russian composer of The Rite of Spring, The Firebird, and Petrushka for Diaghilev's Ballets Russes.
French eccentric composer of the piano Gymnopedies; precursor to Impressionism and ambient "furniture music."
Full name of the Russian composer of The Rite of Spring, The Firebird, and Petrushka.
Stravinsky's ballet for the Ballets Russes whose 1913 premiere caused a riot with its primitivist rhythms.
Ravel's orchestral work built on a single repeating melody over an insistent snare-drum rhythm.
Stravinsky's breakthrough ballet for the Ballets Russes, based on Russian folk tales of a magical bird.
Full name of the French composer of Bolero and Daphnis et Chloe.
Debussy's orchestral triptych evoking the sea's changing moods through shimmering tone color.
Stravinsky's ballet for the Ballets Russes about a puppet who comes to life at a Russian fair.
Debussy's shimmering piano piece from the Suite bergamasque, inspired by a Verlaine poem.
Ravel's suite honoring Baroque forms and friends killed in World War I.
French composer best known for the orchestral scherzo The Sorcerer's Apprentice, popularized by Disney's Fantasia.
Stravinsky's Neoclassical choral symphony setting Latin psalm texts.
Full name of the French Impressionist composer of Prelude to the Afternoon of a Faun.
Ravel's ballet for the Ballets Russes, based on an ancient Greek pastoral romance.
Full name of the French composer best known for The Sorcerer's Apprentice.

entries.topic: Prelude to the Afternoon of a Faun
entries.answerline: Prelude to the Afternoon of a Faun
entries.note: Debussy's orchestral work inspired by a Mallarme poem, a landmark of musical Impressionism.
Dukas's orchestral scherzo depicting an apprentice's enchanted broom disaster, popularized by Fantasia.
Erik Satie's three slow, spare piano pieces, precursors to musical Impressionism and ambient music.
Debussy's piano suite written for his young daughter, including "Golliwogg's Cakewalk."
Full name of the French eccentric composer of the piano Gymnopedies.
Ravel's gently melancholic piano and orchestral work, despite its title not written for any actual death.
French Symbolist poet whose eclogue inspired Debussy's Prelude to the Afternoon of a Faun.
Erik Satie's extremely repetitive piano piece, marked to be played 840 times in a row.

sections.name: Twentieth-Century Modernism
sections.blurb: Atonality, serialism, and experimentalism from the Second Viennese School through Ives, Bartok's mature works, and the postwar avant-garde.

entries.note: Austrian founder of the Second Viennese School; developed atonal expressionism and twelve-tone technique, taught Berg and Webern.
American modernist known for polytonal, collage-like works like The Unanswered Question and Three Places in New England.
Soviet composer of 15 symphonies and string quartets, often read as coded commentary on Stalinist repression.
French modernist inspired by birdsong and Catholic mysticism; wrote the Quartet for the End of Time and Turangalila-Symphonie.
German modernist composer and violist known for a Neoclassical style; wrote the opera Mathis der Maler.
American experimental composer known for chance operation, the prepared piano, and the silent piece 4'33".
American composer known for the elegiac Adagio for Strings, originally the slow movement of a string quartet.
Soviet composer of Peter and the Wolf, Romeo and Juliet, and the satirical Classical Symphony.
Hungarian-Austrian avant-garde composer known for dense tone clusters and micropolyphony, used in 2001: A Space Odyssey.
Russian composer moving from Romantic harmony toward mystic, synesthetic works like Prometheus and the Poem of Ecstasy.
Polish avant-garde composer known for dense tone-cluster works like Threnody to the Victims of Hiroshima.
French composer in Les Six, known for witty Neoclassicism alongside serious sacred works like the Gloria.
German avant-garde composer of electronic and serial music, a pioneer of musique concrete and spatial composition.
Austrian Second Viennese School composer of the operas Wozzeck and Lulu and a Violin Concerto.
German composer and educator best known for the cantata Carmina Burana.
Carl Orff's cantata setting medieval poems, opening with the famous "O Fortuna" chorus.
Group of early-20th-century French composers, including Poulenc and Milhaud, reacting against Impressionism.
Austrian Second Viennese School composer known for extremely concise, pointillistic twelve-tone works.
Common-link Shakespearean subject set as Prokofiev's ballet, Tchaikovsky's fantasy overture, and Berlioz's symphony.
Swiss-French member of Les Six known for the locomotive-depicting orchestral work Pacific 231.
French member of Les Six known for polytonal works influenced by jazz and Brazilian music.
Greek-French avant-garde composer and architect who applied mathematical models to works like Metastaseis.
French composer-conductor and leading voice of postwar serialism, also a champion of new music as conductor.
Japanese avant-garde composer blending Western modernism with traditional Japanese instruments and aesthetics.
American film composer known for the shrieking strings of Psycho and scores for Vertigo and Citizen Kane.
French-American avant-garde composer of percussion-heavy works like Ionisation, a pioneer of electronic music.
American modernist composer known for rhythmic complexity and metric modulation in a long career of chamber works.

entries.topic: Quartet for the End of Time
entries.answerline: Quartet for the End of Time
entries.note: Olivier Messiaen's chamber work composed and premiered while he was a prisoner of war.
Full name of the Soviet composer of 15 symphonies often read as coded commentary on Stalinist repression.
American avant-garde composer known for extended instrumental techniques, as in the string work Black Angels.
French composer and the only woman among Les Six, active in Neoclassical Parisian circles.
Italian avant-garde composer known for the collage-technique orchestral work Sinfonia.
Schoenberg's melodrama for reciter and chamber ensemble, pioneering the vocal technique Sprechstimme.
American film composer known for the scores to Star Wars, Jaws, and Indiana Jones.
Full name of the Soviet composer of Peter and the Wolf and Romeo and Juliet.
Chinese-American composer blending Eastern and Western idioms, known for the film score to Crouching Tiger, Hidden Dragon.
Schoenberg's early, still-tonal string sextet inspired by a Richard Dehmel poem of forgiveness.
Compositional method treating all twelve chromatic pitches equally, devised by Schoenberg and used by Berg and Webern.
Full name of the Russian composer who moved from Romantic harmony toward mystic, synesthetic works.
Full name of the American modernist known for polytonal, collage-like works like The Unanswered Question.
Swiss-American composer known for Jewish-themed works like the cello rhapsody Schelomo.
American composer known for rigorous total serialism and pioneering electronic music studio work.
Genre built from recorded and manipulated real-world sounds, pioneered by Pierre Schaeffer.
Prokofiev's narrated symphonic tale in which each character is represented by a different instrument.
Vocal technique between speech and song pioneered by Schoenberg in Pierrot Lunaire.
Charles Ives's polytonal work layering a solo trumpet's repeated question over quiet string chords.
Messiaen's massive orchestral work on themes of love, joy, and time, featuring the ondes Martenot.
Kubrick film whose soundtrack famously uses Strauss's Also sprach Zarathustra and Ligeti's tone clusters.
Russian composer known for "polystylism," freely mixing tonal and atonal idioms and historical styles.
Ives's piano sonata paying tribute to the Concord, Massachusetts transcendentalists Emerson, Hawthorne, and Thoreau.
American-Mexican composer known for rhythmically impossible studies written for player piano.
Italian film composer famous for spaghetti western scores, including for The Good, the Bad and the Ugly.
Austrian-American composer who brought late-Romantic style to classic Hollywood film scores.
City besieged in World War II, the inspiration for Shostakovich's defiant Symphony No. 7.
British avant-garde composer who later served as Master of the Queen's Music.
Compositional technique organizing pitch, and sometimes other parameters, into fixed ordered series.
Charles Ives's dense, polytonal symphony layering multiple simultaneous musical ideas.
Dense simultaneous groupings of adjacent pitches, used by Ligeti, Cowell, and Penderecki.
American composer known for the synthesizer album Switched-On Bach, popularizing electronic classical music.
John Cage's conceptual work consisting entirely of silence, foregrounding ambient sound.
Samuel Barber's elegiac orchestral work, originally the slow movement of a string quartet.
Charles Ives's atmospheric orchestral work layering quiet strings against sudden ragtime intrusions.
Prokofiev's ballet retelling the fairy tale of a mistreated stepdaughter and a magical transformation.
American pianist closely associated with John Cage, premiering many of his experimental works.
American modernist writer who wrote the libretto for Virgil Thomson's opera Four Saints in Three Acts.
German postwar composer known for a wide-ranging, politically engaged operatic and orchestral output.
French 20th-century composer known for refined orchestral color and slow-evolving forms.
American experimental composer who pioneered tone clusters and playing directly on piano strings.
Subject of Penderecki's dense orchestral memorial work, Threnody to the Victims of Hiroshima.
Edgard Varese's pioneering work scored entirely for percussion instruments and sirens.
Full name of the American experimental composer known for chance operation and the prepared piano.
Polish modernist composer known for richly chromatic, exoticist orchestral and vocal works.
Early 20th-century style returning to Classical-era clarity and form, associated with Stravinsky.
Prolific Soviet symphonist sometimes called the "father of the Soviet symphony."
American experimental composer known for accordion improvisation and the practice of "deep listening."
Circle of Schoenberg, Berg, and Webern who developed atonality and twelve-tone technique in early 20th-century Vienna.
Penderecki's avant-garde setting of the Passion narrative, blending tone clusters with sacred tradition.
Shostakovich's cycle of 15 string quartets, often intensely personal in tone.
Charles Ives's orchestral triptych layering Americana quotations in a dense, polytonal style.

entries.topic: Threnody to the Victims of Hiroshima
entries.answerline: Threnody to the Victims of Hiroshima
entries.note: Penderecki's dense string work of extended techniques memorializing the atomic bombing.
American composer and the first winner of the Pulitzer Prize for Music.
Polish avant-garde composer known for controlled aleatory technique and richly colored orchestration.

sections.name: Minimalism and Contemporary Classical
sections.blurb: Repetitive structures and living composers, from Reich and Glass's minimalism to present-day concert-hall composers.

entries.note: American minimalist composer of Einstein on the Beach and Akhnaten, built on repetitive, additive rhythmic structures.
Late-20th-century movement built on repetitive, slowly shifting patterns, pioneered by Reich, Glass, and Riley.
American minimalist composer of Music for 18 Musicians and Different Trains, known for phasing techniques.
Estonian composer of the "holy minimalist" style called tintinnabuli, as in Spiegel im Spiegel.
American composer of the operas Nixon in China and Doctor Atomic, working in a post-minimalist style.
Full name of the American composer of Nixon in China, working in a post-minimalist style.
American minimalist composer whose In C helped launch the minimalist movement through open, repeating modules.
Contemporary post-minimalist composer known for reworking Vivaldi's Four Seasons and ambient-classical crossovers.
Henryk Gorecki's Symphony No. 3, a slow, meditative "holy minimalist" work that became a surprise bestseller.
Philip Glass's plotless, hours-long opera built on repetitive structures, created with director Robert Wilson.
Finnish contemporary composer known for spectral, atmospheric orchestral and operatic works.
American composer and early minimalist pioneer known for sustained drones and durational works.
Steve Reich's work for string quartet and tape juxtaposing childhood train trips with Holocaust-era transports.
Philip Glass's minimalist opera on the heretical Egyptian pharaoh who imposed monotheism.
Atmospheric, texture-focused genre related to minimalism, pioneered by Brian Eno.
Terry Riley's landmark work built from short repeating modules played in loose synchrony, launching minimalism.
Contemporary American composer awarded the Pulitzer Prize for her Violin Concerto.
Contemporary American string quartet devoted to commissioning and performing new music.
Russian-Tatar composer known for spiritually charged, avant-garde contemporary works.
Contemporary British composer known for works including the opera The Exterminating Angel.
Subject of contemporary memorial works, including John Adams's On the Transmigration of Souls.
American new-music collective and festival promoting contemporary and minimalist-influenced composers.
American composer and the first woman to win the Pulitzer Prize for Music.
Finnish contemporary composer-conductor known for leading major American orchestras and his own orchestral works.
Polish composer whose "holy minimalist" Symphony No. 3 became a surprise bestseller.
Full name of the Polish composer of the "holy minimalist" Symphony No. 3.
American contemporary composer known for the fanfare series Fanfare for the Uncommon Woman.
Contemporary American composer known for the AIDS-themed Symphony No. 1.
American composer and Bang on a Can co-founder, awarded the Pulitzer Prize for Music.
Steve Reich's landmark minimalist work for a mixed ensemble built on pulsing, interlocking rhythms.
John Adams's opera dramatizing Richard Nixon's historic 1972 visit to China.
Contemporary Argentine-American composer known for the multicultural choral-orchestral work La Pasion segun San Marcos.
Steve Reich's compositional technique of gradually shifting identical repeating patterns out of sync.
Arvo Part's spare, meditative piece in his "holy minimalist" tintinnabuli style.
Contemporary South Korean composer known for vividly colored, virtuosic orchestral works.

sections.name: Jazz and American Vernacular Music
sections.blurb: Ragtime, blues, and jazz from Armstrong and Ellington through bebop, cool jazz, and fusion, alongside American musical theater and popular song.

entries.note: American conductor-composer of the musical West Side Story; longtime New York Philharmonic director and Young People's Concerts host.
American composer of the ballets Appalachian Spring and Rodeo, blending folk idioms into an accessible "Americana" style.
American jazz trumpeter and bandleader central to cool jazz and fusion; led the sessions for Kind of Blue.
American composer who fused jazz idioms with concert music in Rhapsody in Blue and the opera Porgy and Bess.
American jazz bandleader-composer and pianist celebrated for orchestral big-band works like Mood Indigo.
American ragtime composer-pianist known for "Maple Leaf Rag" and the opera Treemonisha.
American jazz saxophonist known for A Love Supreme and modal and free jazz innovations.
American jazz trumpeter and singer, a foundational figure of New Orleans jazz known for "West End Blues."
American jazz bassist-composer-bandleader known for blending bebop, gospel, and classical elements.
American "March King" who led the U.S. Marine Band and wrote "The Stars and Stripes Forever."
American alto saxophonist and pioneer of bebop, known by the nickname "Bird."
American jazz trumpeter and bebop pioneer known for his bent-bell trumpet and Afro-Cuban jazz fusions.
American jazz pianist who moved from Miles Davis's quintet into jazz fusion and funk.
George Gershwin's jazz-influenced concert work for piano and orchestra, opening with a famous clarinet glissando.
American jazz pianist known for the unusual time signatures of "Take Five" and the album Time Out.
American saxophonist and pioneer of free jazz, known for the album The Shape of Jazz to Come.
Syncopated American piano genre popularized by Scott Joplin, a direct precursor to jazz.
American jazz pianist-composer known for angular melodies and dissonant harmony, as in "'Round Midnight."
Miles Davis's landmark modal jazz album, one of the best-selling jazz records ever made.
American saxophonist-composer who played with Miles Davis and co-founded the fusion group Weather Report.
John Coltrane's spiritually themed suite, widely regarded as a landmark of modal and spiritual jazz.
American jazz harpist and pianist, John Coltrane's wife, known for spiritually inflected modal jazz.
American clarinetist and bandleader known as the "King of Swing" during the big-band era.
American jazz vocalist known for her expressive phrasing and the protest song "Strange Fruit."
Relaxed, understated jazz style of the 1950s associated with Miles Davis and the West Coast scene.
American trumpeter, composer, and bandleader who champions traditional jazz repertoire at Jazz at Lincoln Center.
American swing-era clarinetist and bandleader known for "Begin the Beguine."
American jazz pianist known for the fusion group Return to Forever and long collaboration with Miles Davis.
Genre blending jazz improvisation with rock and funk elements, pioneered by Miles Davis and Weather Report.
Early New Orleans jazz pianist-composer who claimed, controversially, to have invented jazz.
American city considered the birthplace of jazz, home to early figures like Louis Armstrong and Jelly Roll Morton.
African American religious folk songs that fed into gospel, blues, and jazz traditions.
Dave Brubeck Quartet's album exploring unusual meters, home to the hit "Take Five."
African American composer of the Afro-American Symphony, blending blues and jazz idioms into concert music.
Jazz fusion standard composed by Joe Zawinul for the band Weather Report.
American jazz pianist known for impressionistic harmony, a key voice on Miles Davis's Kind of Blue.
Full name of the American jazz bandleader-composer known for orchestral big-band works.
Common first name referring to jazz vocalist Ella Fitzgerald, celebrated for scat singing.
Common first name referring to jazz trumpeter Miles Davis.
Full name of the American jazz trumpeter and bandleader behind Kind of Blue.
Miles Davis and Gil Evans's orchestral jazz album drawing on Spanish and flamenco idioms.
American jazz tenor saxophonist known for the album Saxophone Colossus and extended improvisations.
19th-century American songwriter of parlor and minstrel songs like "Oh! Susanna," a forerunner of popular song.
Full name of the American jazz pianist-composer known for angular melodies and dissonant harmony.
Jazz standard composed by Thelonious Monk, among the most recorded tunes in jazz history.

sections.name: Instruments, Forms, and Musical Institutions
sections.blurb: Individual instruments, generic musical forms and theory terms, and the orchestras, halls, cities, and performers tied to classical music life.
